items.correct: The sudden release of built-up stress along faults at plate boundaries as rocks slip past each other, generating seismic waves.
items.misconception: Earthquakes are caused by magma rising up to the surface.
items.random_wrong: Earthquakes are caused by the Earth's core overheating.
items.why: Students conflate volcanic and seismic processes. Most earthquakes are tectonic (stress release at faults), not volcanic — magma movement is a separate process and most quakes occur at non-volcanic boundaries.

items.correct: When stressed (usually by warming), corals expel their symbiotic zooxanthellae (algae that provide colour and food), turning white. They can recover if stress eases, but die if it persists.
items.misconception: Coral bleaching is when coral is killed by sunlight bleaching its colour.
items.random_wrong: Coral bleaching is when boats spill chemicals that whiten the reef.
items.why: It's the loss of algal symbionts, not sun-fading. Bleached coral is not yet dead — it's a warning state. Repeated mass bleaching (like 2016 on the Great Barrier Reef) causes long-term reef collapse.

items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).
Correct answer: Myers. “E.O. Wilson” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Tropical rainforest. “Desert” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 36 hotspots. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
